title: Booting from a vinyl record (2020)
author: yesturi
summary:
BOGIN, JR.                        

Most PCs tend to boot from a primary media storage, be it a hard disk drive, or a solid-state drive, perhaps from a network, or – if all else fails – the USB stick or the boot DVD comes to the rescue… Fun, eh? Boring! Why don’t we try to boot from a record player for a change?
64 512 byte DOS boot disk on a 10″ record, total playing time 06:10 on 45 rpm

Update February 2022: Click here to observe the very same vinyl ramdisk booted on an IBM PCjr!
So this nutty little experiment connects a PC, or an IBM PC to be exact, directly onto a record player through an amplifier. I made a small ROM on-chip boot loader that operates the built-in “cassette interface” of the PC (that was hardly ever used), which will now be invoked by the BIOS if all the other boot options fail, i.e. floppy disk and the hard drive. The turntable spins an analog recording of a small bootable read-only RAM drive, which is 64K in size. This contains a FreeDOS kernel, modified by me to cram it into the memory constraint, a micro variant of COMMAND.COM and a patched version of INTERLNK, that allows file transfer through a printer cable, modified to be runnable on FreeDOS. The bootloader reads the disk image from the audio recording through the cassette modem, loads it to memory and boots the system on it. Simple huh?
The vinyl loader code, in a ROM
(It can also reside on a hard drive or a floppy, but that’d be cheating)
And now to get more technical: this is basically a merge between BootLPT/86 and 5150CAXX, minus the printer port support. It also resides in a ROM, in the BIOS expansion socket, but it does not have to. The connecting cable between the PC and the record player amplifier is the same as with 5150CAXX, just without the line-in (PC data out) jack.
The “cassette interface” itself is just PC speaker timer channel 2 for the output, and 8255A-5 PPI port C channel 4 (PC4, I/O port 62h bit 4) for the input. BIOS INT 15h routines are used for software (de)modulation.
The boot image is the same 64K BOOTDISK.IMG “example” RAM drive that can be downloaded at the bottom of the BootLPT article. This has been turned into an “IBM cassette tape”-protocol compliant audio signal using 5150CAXX, and sent straight to a record cutting lathe.
Vinyls are cut with an RIAA equalization curve that a preamp usually reverses during playback, but not perfectly. So some signal correction had to be applied from the amplifier, as I couldn’t make it work right with the line output straight from the phono preamp. In my case, involving a vintage Harman&Kardon 6300 amplifier with an integrated MM phono preamp, I had to fade the treble all the way down to -10dB/10kHz, increase bass equalization to approx. +6dB/50Hz and reduce the volume level to approximately 0.7 volts peak, so it doesn’t distort. All this, naturally, with any phase and loudness correction turned off.
Of course, the cassette modem does not give a hoot in hell about where the signal is coming from. Notwithstanding, the recording needs to be pristine and contain no pops or loud crackles (vinyl) or modulation/frequency drop-outs (tape) that will break the data stream from continuing. However, some wow is tolerated, and the speed can be 2 or 3 percent higher or lower too.
Bootloader in a ROM; being an EPROM for a good measure
And that’s it! For those interested, the bootloader binary designed for a 2364 chip (2764s can be used, through an adaptor), can be obtained here. It assumes an IBM 5150 with a monochrome screen and at least 512K of RAM, which kind of reminds me of my setup (what a coincidence). The boot disk image can be obtained at the bottom of the BootLPT/86 article, and here’s its analog variant, straight from the grooves 🙂
Genau mein Humor !!!111!1
Du bist ein Nerd-Endboss!
Life the Retro, very cool cool project thanks for doing it.
btw, have you reverse engineered the intersrv/interlnk protocol
or are able to reverse it? it would be very cool to have an intersrv
windows or linux server (and a DOS client).
Size-wise, do you think it would be possible to cram a 3COM packet driver and tFTP implementation in the same EPROM? There don’t appear to be any netboot/etherboot implementations that work on an 8088, as they expect to download a whole floppy disk image into an extended memory ramdisk requiring at least a 286 processor.
Certainly not within 8K, which is what the 5150 expects in its expansion socket, however, inside a 27512 (64K) in a dedicated NIC might be a different story. I have an “Earthstation” V20-based diskless workstation that contains its own BIOS inside the same 64K EPROM as its bootable ArcNET Novell Netware + CBIS Network O/S network stacks. Sure, that predates Ethernet, however, the same rig was later offered with an Ethernet stack.
So you are still using the IBM BIOS provided INT 15h routines for the cassette, and your boot loader ROM is hooking INT 19h for IPL and you have a second boot loader that creates a RAM disk (C: ?) and writes the FreeDOS boot files there?  Pretty slick.
If that’s the case, any system BIOS that supports INT 15h cassette could also work as well.  Have you tried GLaBIOS (glabios.org)?  It’s an open source BIOS that supports cassette on original PC too.
It’s actually only one bootloader, the one I put in the BIOS extension ROM. It allocates a 64K segment for the vinyl record data at RAM top, which is then deducted from the available memory, before passing control to DOS. It also installs an INT 13h handler so that it simulates floppy read accesses on said data segment (check out the BootLPT/86 code); the physical A: floppy drive, if present, is set to function as B:. DOS itself does not know it is a RAM drive.
Right before boot, the first 512 bytes at the loading location (the boot sector) is copied to the standard 0000:7C00h location and a jmp far is done, simulating BIOS INT 19h. The interrupt itself is not used – actually, anyone (or anything) invoking INT 19h would cause the BIOS to read the 1st sector from the physical A: floppy, doing a normal boot.
I did not test GlaBIOS as I don’t have the BIOS ROM chip in the 5150 socketed, and I only have one single 2364-to-2764 adaptor for the option ROM socket.
would it be able to modify to make use of the audio in on the sound blaster and compatible cards?
The disassembly of the IBM PC BIOS is freely available, incl. the software cassette modem, utilizing the programmable interval timer for the “audio” input. If you have code access to the sound card input, you should be able to implement a simple demodulation routine.
Fascinante seu trabalho em demostrar como o DOS é possível ser iniciado pelo disco de Vinil nunca imaginei que realmente seria possível tal procedimento, já consegui dar o boot do Ms-DOS 6.22 apartir de um USBDRIVE usando o MULTIBOOT YUMI em um pc de 64 bits foi muito legal.
Toš ďakujem za pochvalu 🙂 Užitočné to moc neni, ale jeden sa pri tom zabaví. DOS 6.22 do toho image, žiaľ, nezmestím. Kvôli realmode segmentácii je maximálna veľkosť image obmedzená BIOSom na 64K a to hneď zožerú pochutiny ako IO.SYS, MSDOS.SYS, COMMAND.COM…
Very interesting, well done and well explained!
Thank´s for sharing!
Love this! It’s basically the same as a cassette but on record.
Just one question: How did you manage to copy the information to the vinyl? To my knowledge this is far from straight forward.
CU Felix
I have a turntable connected to my PC through a USB can it be used? Where can I get the vinyl?
Great work and the sound reminded me my old ZX Spectrum days
wow. intriguing. should’ve required hours of your time. all the best.
This is so cool. Love it. Geeky dumb ass that I am, I cannot resist the challenge. I must (must, must, must) do this using not the turntable (similar to the Audio Tecknica that I own), but an Edison gramophone.
You can add some error correction to the encoding to help deal with pop and crackle.
A simple scheme will fit very snugly as a decoder as well, but those details depend upon the actual distribution of the errors.
I LOVE this.
Further possibilities: allow boot off the microphone input.  Learn to whistle, sing a song, or conduct an orchestra, the resulting melody of which encodes the kernel.  Bonus points if you can make it work to the tune of “Inna gadda da vida”.
Great!!! I would also give the option to boot using “metal machine music” of Lou Reed: https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=XIMSbKU2oZM
Didn’t the stock 5150 ROM have cassette support, but only via BASIC? If it didn’t find a bootable floppy it dropped you into IBM cassette BASIC. 
Most* of the PC lineup sold by IBM would go into Cassette BASIC if no bootable disk was found including some members of the PS/2 lineup. Only the 5150, PC Jr, and JX (improved PC Jr for Japan, Australia and New Zealand) were PCs from IBM with cassette ports. 
All 3 machines had INT 15h BIOS support for the cassette port which any program could have used. BASIC did it by default and there were a couple of non-BASIC programs that also called INT 15h cassette routines. 5150CAXX does it. There were also some BASICODE related programs that would use BIOS INT 15h for handling files at normal IBM cassette
speeds in addition to having their own modified cassette routines to deal with the BASICODE cassette format.
* I am not checking if something like the XT/370 with mainframe cards would fall back into ROM BASIC.
Fantastic stuff. Why was a custom BIOS necessary. Did the original IBM PC BIOS not support the cassette interface?
The BIOS is completely stock and has not been modified. However, the ROM chip that you see, was plugged into the empty “expansion socket” next to the BIOS chips, to override the PC boot process. This allowed the PC to try booting from a floppy/hard drive first. And only after that failed, the vinyl boot was offered – whose code is a part of the ROM.
Of course, the vinyl bootloader would happily run from a disk drive. However, that would be considered cheating, as the PC would need to boot from a disk first – only to offer vinyl booting afterwards.
There’s also been a recent MS-DOS related minimal techno release.
You can find more info here:
https://www.discogs.com/MS-DOS-CD-Dir/release/16091942
The content of the record here, with the stickers of the other release would look pretty amazing.
Next up: fax machine!
Print out the kernel on to paper, feed it via fax to the cassette port.
so if you press the TURBO button can you play back the vinyl record at 45 RPM? 😉
Hi,
this is nuts, you are awesome for doing well. One question though, how did you press the vinyl? Could you please release its content as a 1411 kbit/s FLAC file? 
Yes, the FLAC can be downloaded in the last paragraph of the article; the vinyl has been cut by a third party.
Awesome job dude! Wish I could do that on my PC-XT!
just because you can, doesnt mean you  should. or does it? 🙂
simply awesome!
Recorded for posterity
https://www.reddit.com/r/technology/comments/jyvqiv/booting_from_a_vinyl_record/
As a programmer by day, and turntablist by night; this tickles both my nerd fancies hilariously. You rock for doing this.
I have a very serious question, though; from the turntablism side – how the heck did you get a press of that? I imagine obviously getting the source 
Is it just a dubplate? Do you have a friend with a lathe or something? It’s the #1 lingering question in my mind post reading this awesome article.
Oh, one more thing – is there enough volume differential between the cassette and the vinyl that the signal modification is necessary? 
Would doing these signal modifications (amplitude aside, if the medium prohibits it) on the recording manually using an equalizer, etc; before it is pressed to the vinyl reduce the need for this post signal modification? Just curious.
I remember similar functionality although also on tape on early Tandy.
Can you imagine in the middle of ’80 a Polish GOVERNMENT radio station broadcasted programs for ZX Spectrum, Atari, Amiga to the air?
“Your taperecorders on: 3, 2, 1, go!”
There were such broadcasts in the 1980s in East Germany. The official youth radio station named DT64 had a show called Computer Club and they were broadcasting homecomputer programms over the airwaves so enthusiast could tape them. And there were vinyl editions for those who missed the transmissions.
That happened in Sweden too. I don’t think I managed to get the programs running though.
There were broadcasts of Spectrum games from the national broadcaster in Ireland too around that time – I had a Spectrum 128K +2. Might have been 1986/1987
Thanks a lot for the story. Takes me back to the first Sinclair ZX -81 (?) computer kit that I built in my attic, and then later with my VIC 64 using cassette of course.
If you play the record backwards, will it boot Windows 95?
It would boot Inferno OS
https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Inferno_(operating_system)
Did you use a homemade vinyl lathe? Curious if one can be accurate for this type of dense signal!
Record lathes don’t typically cut vinyl, even though everyone calls it vinyl because mass-produced records are pressed from vinyl. Is it nitrocellulose lacquer or polycarbonate?
(Asking because I have cut records myself.)

content:
# Booting a PC from a Vinyl Record – The Out‑of‑the‑Box Experiment That Really Works  

*Original post on Hacker News: “Booting from a vinyl record (2020)” by yesturi (score 175).*  

---

## What the project is all about  

Most of us are used to starting a computer from a hard‑drive, SSD, USB stick or a DVD.  
yesturi decided to ask a stranger question: **“Can a PC be booted from a phonograph?”**  
The answer is **yes** – and the method is a clever blend of old‑school cassette‑modem tricks, a tiny ROM bootloader and a specially‑cut vinyl record that stores a 64 KB RAM‑disk image containing a tiny FreeDOS system.

> *“Why not spin a record to load the operating system? Boring‑free!”*  

---

## How it works (in plain English)

| Step | What happens |
|------|--------------|
| **1. Data → audio** | The 64 KB boot image (`BOOTDISK.IMG`) is converted into an audio signal using the same modulation that IBM’s cassette interface used back in the early 80 s. |
| **2. Vinyl cutting** | The audio is sent to a record‑cutting lathe and pressed onto a 10‑inch, 45 RPM vinyl disc. The whole disc holds about 6 minutes of sound – enough for the whole boot image. |
| **3. Connect the record player** | The turntable’s output goes through a vintage Harman Kardon 6300 amp (with MM phono pre‑amp) into the PC’s cassette input line (8255 PPI port C‑bit 4). |
| **4. ROM bootloader** | A tiny boot ROM (2364 EPROM) is plugged into the IBM 5150’s expansion slot. It intercepts the normal BIOS boot flow (INT 19h) and, if floppy/hard‑drive boot fails, activates the cassette‑modem routines (INT 15h). |
| **5. Demodulation** | The BIOS cassette routines treat the audio coming from the record exactly like cassette data: they demodulate the signal, copy the 64 KB into RAM, and jump to the boot sector at 0 7C00h. |
| **6. FreeDOS runs** | The loaded image contains a stripped‑down FreeDOS kernel, a minimal `COMMAND.COM`, and a patched `INTERLNK` driver that lets the system communicate over a printer‑cable‑style link. The PC boots into a functional DOS environment. |

### Key hardware bits

* **IBM 5150 (or PC‑jr)** – the original machine that still has a cassette port.  
* **Cassette interface** – uses the PC speaker timer (channel 2) for output and PPI port C bit 4 for input.  
* **Custom ROM** – holds the tiny bootloader that calls the BIOS cassette routines.  
* **Vinyl record + amp** – provides the audio “tape” that the modem reads.

---

## Why the audio tweaks mattered  

Vinyl is normally mastered with the **RIAA equalisation curve**. When played back through a phono pre‑amp the curve is reversed, but it’s never *perfect*. To get a clean data stream yesturi had to:

* Roll the treble down to **‑10 dB at 10 kHz**.  
* Boost the bass to about **+6 dB at 50 Hz**.  
* Keep the overall level around **0.7 V peak** to avoid clipping.  

No phase or loudness compensation was used – we want the raw signal that the cassette modem can decode.

---

## What you can try yourself  

Even if you don’t have a vintage IBM PC, the concept is adaptable:

* **Use a modern PC’s sound card** – the PCM input can be used for demodulation (just write a simple software modem).  
* **Create the audio file** – tools like `bootlpt` or `cassetmod` can turn a binary image into a cassette‑style WAV.  
* **Cut a dub‑plate** – a single‑turn lathe cut is enough for testing; you don’t need a mass‑production press.  
* **Play from a USB turntable** – feed the line‑out into the PC’s line‑in (or directly into a sound‑card‑based modem).  

The biggest obstacle is **signal quality**: pops, clicks or extreme wow will break the data stream. A clean recording (or a good‑quality vinyl) is essential.

---

## What we learned

* **Old hardware still has hidden tricks** – the IBM cassette port, long thought obsolete, can still be repurposed.  
* **Data can live in any analog medium** – tape, radio, vinyl… the only limit is how well you can modulate and demodulate it.  
* **A few kilobytes of code go a long way** – the whole bootloader fits in a 2 KB EPROM, yet it lets a 64 KB RAM‑disk run FreeDOS.  

It’s a perfect blend of **retro‑hacking**, audio engineering, and pure geek fun.

---

### Want to dig deeper?

* Download the **bootloader ROM image** (2364 / 2764 EPROM) from the original article.  
* Grab the **64 KB bootdisk image** (`BOOTDISK.IMG`) and the **WAV version** of the vinyl data.  
* Check out the related **BootLPT/86** project for the original cassette‑modem source code.  

---

*Curious how it would sound if you played the record backwards? Maybe a hidden “boot Windows 95” Easter egg! 😄*  

---  

**Source:** [Booting from a vinyl record (2020) – Hacker News discussion](https://boginjr.com/it/sw/dev/vinyl-boot/)   (original article linked above).
